[title] IT

[author_marker] Written 
[author_marker] by
[author_marker] Chase Palmer & Cary Fukunaga

[source_marker] Based on the novel 
[source_marker] by 
[source_marker] Stephen King

[source_names] STUDIO DRAFT 3-18-2014

[notes] Warner Bros.

[action] OPEN ON:

[action] Rain. Lashing a windowpane. A PIANO PLAYS somewhere off 
[action] screen. Beethoven’s  Fur Elise .

[scene_heading] INT. WILL’S BEDROOM - DAY

[action] A little boy gazes out into the storm. Nervous, eager, sweet. 
[action] Meet GEORGE DENBROUGH (9).

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] You sure it’s not too bad out 
[dialogue] there?

[action] Will (13), his older brother, sits up in bed propped against 
[action] a pile of pillows, surrounded by tissues and sheets of 
[action] newspaper. HEADLINES and TOYS tell us this is the late 80’s -- 
[action] October, 1987 to be exact. He puts finishing touches on

[subheading] A PAPER BOAT

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Don’t be such a wuss. I’d come if I 
[dialogue] weren’t dying. Now get the 
[dialogue] paraffin.

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] In the cellar?

[character] WILL
[dialogue] You want it to float or not, 
[dialogue] Georgie? Go on. Storm’s not gonna 
[dialogue] last forever.

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] Okay, Willy.

[action] George jumps up obediently and goes.

[transition] CUT TO:

[action] TITLE CARD: 

[character] OCTOBER 1987

[scene_heading] INT. WILL’S HOUSE - DAY

[action] George hurries downstairs. He catches a glimpse of their 
[action] mother, SHARON DENBROUGH (30s) in the parlor playing piano, 
[action] an earthy beauty transported to another world while she 
[action] plays.

[scene_heading] INT. KITCHEN - WILL’S HOUSE - DAY

[action] George’s stomach sinks as he comes face to face with THE 
[action] CELLAR DOOR -- the only thing between George and the monster 
[action] in the basement of his imagination -- IT.

[scene_heading] INT. WILL’S BEDROOM - CONTINUOUS

[action] A WALKIE-TALKIE SQUAWKS next to Will.  The sarcastic walkie-
[action] talkie filtered voice of RICHIE “TRASHMOUTH” TOZIER (12), his 
[action] neighbor, filters through the plastic speaker.

[character] RICHIE
[dialogue] (walkie-talkie filtered)
[dialogue] Willy-boy.

[action] Will, annoyed, picks up the walkie-talkie.  While looking 
[action] through his rain blasted window.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Trashmouth.

[action] They wave at each other.  Richie, bug-eyed glasses, turns the 
[action] wave into a middle-finger.

[character] RICHIE (O.S.)
[dialogue] Thought you were sick.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] I am.

[character] RICHIE (O.S.)
[dialogue] Is it mortal?

[scene_heading] INT. CELLAR DOORWAY - CONTINUOUS

[action] Flinging it open, George ventures his arm into the DARK VOID. 
[action] He gropes around and finds the LIGHT SWITCH. Nothing. George 
[action] snatches his arm back.
[page_number] 2.

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] Oh, balls. The power.

[action] The dark basement glares back at him, taunting.

[action] ***HERE WE DO SOME SORT OF CALL FROM WILL UPSTAIRS THAT IS A 
[action] “CODE” THEY DO WITH EACH OTHER.  THIS WILL COME BACK TO HAUNT 
[action] US LATER.***

[scene_heading] INT. WILL’S BEDROOM - CONTINUOUS

[action] ***GEORGIE RESPONDS WITH HIS OWN CALL FROM DOWNSTAIRS***

[character] RICHIE (O.S.)
[dialogue] What was that?

[action] Will coughs hard into a tissue. Half-impatient, half-joking:

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Nothing.  Georgie and I are making 
[dialogue] a paper boat.  Got to go-

[character] RICHIE
[dialogue] Wait-!

[action] Will turns off the walkie-talkie.

[scene_heading] INT. KITCHEN - WILL’S HOUSE - CONTINUOUS

[action] George rubs the gooseflesh on his arms.

[character] GEORGE
[parenthetical] (to himself)
[dialogue] I’m getting it.  I’m going to get 
[dialogue] it.  I’m going in.  I’m...

[action] He steels himself and plunges into the void.

[scene_heading] INT. CELLAR - WILL’S HOUSE - CONTINUOUS

[action] George scrambles down four steps to THE CELLAR SHELF and 
[action] sifts through junk as fast as he can: SHOE-POLISH, RAGS, a 
[action] dusty bag of colored BALLOONS, a broken FLASHLIGHT, two 
[action] mostly empty bottles of WINDEX, and an old can of TURTLE WAX. 

[action] George stops and gazes upon the CARTOON TURTLE with hypnotic 
[action] wonder. CUT TO POV of something lurking in recesses of the 
[action] basement, watching GEORGE framed in the light of the doorway.
[page_number] 3.

[action] Sensing this, George snaps out of it, grabs the BOX OF 
[action] PARAFFIN near the back of the shelf, and hurries back up the 
[action] stairs, slamming the door in our/It’s face.

[scene_heading] INT. WILL’S BEDROOM - DAY

[action] Will melts a chunk of paraffin with a match in a ceramic 
[action] bowl, then dips his finger into the hot liquid and smears the 
[action] wax along the sides of the boat.

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] Can I do some?

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Okay. Just don’t get any on my 
[dialogue] blankets or Mom’ll freak.

[action] George dips his finger and spreads it.

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] Too cool.

[action] They finish up and Will gives the boat to George.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] There you go. She’s all yours.

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] She?

[character] WILL
[dialogue] You’re the Captain, ain’t ya? 
[dialogue] Captains call their boats she. Now 
[dialogue] put on your rain stuff or you’ll 
[dialogue] wind up stuck in bed like your 
[dialogue] stupid brother.

[action] They both grin, the cozy room full of cheerful brotherly 
[action] love. George kisses Will on the cheek, startling him.

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] Thanks, Willy.

[action] He goes. Will looks to the rain-lashed window, piano still 
[action] playing. He’s suddenly filled with a sense of foreboding.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Be careful out there!
[page_number] 4.

[scene_heading] EXT. WITCHAM STREET - DAY

[action] A DEAD TRAFFIC LIGHT sways overhead, its dripping black 
[action] lenses gazing back at A BOY IN A YELLOW SLICKER AND RED 
[action] GALOSHES.

[action] Georgie races down the street past dark houses after his 
[action] PAPER BOAT, which sluices along a gutter swollen with rushing 
[action] rainwater.

[action] Rain taps on George’s hood sounding to his ears like rain on 
[action] a shed roof, a comforting almost cozy sound. The buckles of 
[action] his galoshes make a merry jingle as he goes.

[action] The boat whistles past a blockade of sawhorses marked  DEPT OF 
[action] DERRY PUBLIC WORKS,  where a gouge in the blacktop sends it 
[action] sweeping diagonally across toward a STORM DRAIN.

[action] George races after, trying to grab the boat before it’s 
[action] swallowed up with all the rainwater. He SLIPS AND SPILLS to 
[action] the blacktop, crying out in pain.

[action] The PAPER BOAT surfs up to the drain, circles around twice, 
[action] and goes in. George look ready to cry.

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] Willy’s gonna kill me.

[action] He walks over to the drain and peers in, water falling into 
[action] darkness. A dank hollow sound comes from within. Suddenly

[action] EYES PEER BACK AT HIM -- from THE GREASY WHITE FACE OF A 
[action] CLOWN.  Not Bozo, or Ronald McDonald, but something more old 
[action] world, freakish, like that of a 19th-century acrobat -- bald, 
[action] lithe, almost child-like. Meet PENNYWISE aka BOB GRAY.

[action] George recoils back, shocked by Pennywise’s presence. A 
[action] VOICE, a perfectly pleasant and reasonable voice, rises up.

[character] PENNYWISE (O.C.)
[dialogue] Hello, Georgie.

[action] George looks around him. 

[action] An OLD WOMAN watches him from the kitchen window of a house 
[action] just behind the drain. 

[action] CUT TO: POV from the OLD WOMAN’s house.  She turns her 
[action] attention back to her cat, scraping out the wet innards of a 
[action] can of tuna into a plate on the window sill.

[character] PENNYWISE (O.C.) (CONT’D)
[dialogue] Lost something?
[page_number] 5.

[action] George expression mellows from fear to child-like wonder, 
[action] like he’s seeing a circus bear balanced on a ball .

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] How did you get down there?

[character] PENNYWISE
[dialogue] Me? Why the storm of course. Just 
[dialogue] bleeeew me away. It blew the whole 
[dialogue] circus away. How’d you like to join 
[dialogue] the circus, Georgie?

[action] The faint, off-key sound of CALLIOPE MUSIC can be heard. 
[action] George inches closer to the storm drain.

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] Is there cotton candy?

[character] PENNYWISE
[dialogue] Cotton candy? Oh yes. Cotton candy 
[dialogue] and bearded women and elephant shit 
[dialogue] and all the balloons a little boy 
[dialogue] could want. You like balloons, 
[dialogue] don’t you Georgie?

[character] GEORGE
[dialogue] I sure do. Do they float?

[action] George takes one last fatal step forward. 

[character] PENNYWISE
[dialogue] Float? Oh indeed they do, Georgie. 
[dialogue] Indeed they do. Everything down 
[dialogue] here floats. And when you’re down 
[dialogue] here with us...

[action] The clown’s sing-songy voice curdles into something horrible, 
[action] primal even, as George’s arm is seized by Pennywise’s 
[action] unusually LONG arm and CLAW-like hand.

[character] PENNYWISE (CONT’D)
[dialogue] YOU’LL FLOAT TOO!

[action] George’s SCREEEEEAAAAAM rings through the sewers as we...

[action] CUT TO the OLD WOMAN’s POV again.  The cat devours its food, 
[action] the old woman pleased with his appetite does not notice... 

[action] RACK FOCUS... Georgie’s rag doll body flung left and right as 
[action] Pennywise feeds on him and tries to pull him through the 
[action] metal grate into the sewer.

[transition] SMASH CUT TO:
[page_number] 6.

[action] Soaring, percussive MUSIC crescendos over a wide shot of 
[action] glacier-cut mountains.  Then, SILENCE.  

[action] Title card:
[centered] JUNE 1988

[scene_heading] EXT. DERRY - AERIAL - SAME

[action] PAN OFF glacier-cut mountains to reveal the TOWN OF DERRY, 
[action] MAINE, settled on a crosscut of the Penobscot River and 
[action] Kenduskaeg stream. It’s a sturdy, picturesque Northeastern 
[action] town like any other, its rough-hewn industrial past bleeding 
[action] through a gentrified, decaying present.

[action] TILT STRAIGHT DOWN to HANLON ABATOIR

[action] SEVERAL multi-acre, manure filled sheep pens stand empty.  
[action] One has 50 sheep ready for slaughter, a path leading from the 
[action] pen grows narrower as it feeds into an industrial complex 
[action] designed for slaughter.

[scene_heading] EXT. HANLON ABATTOIR, OUTDOOR PEN - DAY

[action] LEROY HANLON (50s) works to move the sheep into line while 
[action] his son MIKE HANLON (13) watches from behind him. 

[character] LEROY
[dialogue] Open the gate.

[character] MIKE
[dialogue] I’m sick of this, dad.

[character] LEROY
[dialogue] People need to eat.  Now open the 
[dialogue] gate.

[action] Mike opens the gate.

[character] MIKE
[dialogue] How would you feel if you were one 
[dialogue] of them? 

[action] Leroy, haunted by this thought answers gruffly- 

[character] LEROY
[dialogue] Humans aren’t raised to be food.
[page_number] 7.

[action] As if he were lying, that humans sometimes  ARE  raised for 
[action] food. 

[action] The sheep flood into a thinner pathway leading to the 
[action] interior of the complex.  Leroy watches them as does Mike.

[character] MIKE
[dialogue] Everyday, watching them stupidly 
[dialogue] walk into that killing pen.  This 
[dialogue] isn’t a normal kid’s life.  You 
[dialogue] won’t even let me go to school.

[scene_heading] INT. HANLON ABATOIR, SLAUGHTER PEN - DAY

[action] Leroy takes the cattle bolt, places it on the head of the 
[action] first sheep.  WOOMPH.  The sheep drops dead.

[character] LEROY
[dialogue] Now why would you want to be in 
[dialogue] public school with the rest of 
[dialogue] those people down below, in Derry?

[action] He hands it to Mike, expectantly.  Mike dispassionately takes 
[action] the mechanism and approaches a sheep.

[character] MIKE
[dialogue] Because I want to be in boring 
[dialogue] classes with other boring kids and 
[dialogue] draw pictures in text books and 
[dialogue] play baseball and go to dances and 
[dialogue] grind. 

[action] He places it on the head of the next sheep, but can’t bring 
[action] himself to pull the trigger.  

[action] Leroy does it for him.  WOOMPH.  The sheep drops dead.

[character] LEROY
[dialogue] I know, son.  I get it.  But you 
[dialogue] aren’t like those people.  We may 
[dialogue] work with sheep, but those people 
[dialogue] are sheep.  Trust me.

[action] The last point Leroy believes in like gospel.

[subheading] A SCHOOL BELL RINGS

[scene_heading] EXT. DERRY MIDDLE SCHOOL - DAY

[action] Doors fling open and ROWDY 6TH, 7TH, and 8TH GRADERS spill 
[action] out. Books are hurled in the trash, papers strewn all over 
[action] the recess yard -- summer is officially commenced. 
[page_number] 8.

[action] Swept up among this madness is... 

[subheading] WILL DENBROUGH (13)

[action] Handsome, gangly, red-headed, the memory of his brother still 
[action] fresh. He walks with his dorky friends, all talking over each 
[action] other: EDDIE KASPBRAK, his EPI-PEN JR. (an adrenaline 
[action] injector for kids with life-threatening allergies) holstered 
[action] in his medicine-filled fannypack; STAN URIS (12), a trim and 
[action] tidy Jewish kid who dresses like a mini-accountant; and 
[action] RICHIE TOZIER, the neighbor with the walkie-talkie, a comic 
[action] book and video game freak with bug-eye glasses. 

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] So how’s it work?

[character (DUAL)] STAN
[dialogue (DUAL)] What work?
[character (DUAL)] RICHIE
[dialogue (DUAL)] So long suckas!

[action] Richie flips off the school.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Richie!

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] The ceremony. Your Bar Mitzvah.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] It makes you officially a man 
[dialogue] right?

[character] STAN
[action] Kind of. I read verses. From 
[action] the Torah.
[dialogue] RICHIE
[dialogue] Then a seventh dimensional 
[dialogue] door opens up and Stan here 
[dialogue] has to slay his Ronin master 
[dialogue] over a pit of death. He wins 
[dialogue] his schlong grows like six 
[dialogue] inches.

[character] STAN
[dialogue] Shit, I don’t need a Bar Mitzvah 
[dialogue] for that. 

[action] Richie howls.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Just your mom’s Cosmo.

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] Marie Claire.
[page_number] 9.

[character] RICHIE
[dialogue] Vogue man. It’s all perfumed. I put 
[dialogue] on my 3-D glasses and it’s like 
[dialogue] you’re there.

[action] Richie “motor boats” the air in front of him.  

[action] ANGLE on a GROUP OF fashionably dressed GIRLS NEARBY.  The 
[action] bigger, heavier one of the group notices someone off screen.

[character] GIRL #1
[dialogue] There’s the ho-bag, now.

[action] PAN to see BEVERLY MARSH (13) a dazzling cloud of auburn hair 
[action] and lovely green-gray eyes. Her clothes aren’t as nice as the 
[action] group of popular girls, but she holds her head high.

[character] GIRL #2
[dialogue] She’s the one all the eighth grade 
[dialogue] boys talk about.

[character] GIRL #1
[action] They say she gives the best 
[action] bjs.
[dialogue] GIRL #1 (CONT’D)
[dialogue] Probably the only way she can 
[dialogue] afford those rags she wears.  
[dialogue] Give me your rings, Gretta.

[action] Beverly sees the girls as Girl #1 puts Gretta’s rings on her 
[action] fingers. Sensing trouble, she turns around and ducks right 
[action] back into school.

[character] GIRL #1
[dialogue] Hey cock breath, come here!

[action] PAN back to WILL and his dorky friends.  Eddie stops dead, 
[action] pointing to-

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] Jesus, she’s still here?

[action] The RABBI’S WIFE, sleepless and desperate, lurks on the edge 
[action] of school property, scanning the throngs of departing kids 
[action] with desperate eyes. Next to her, a FRONT LAWN MARQUEE reads:

[] REMEMBER THE CURFEW.
[centered] 7 P.M.
[centered] DERRY POLICE DEPARTMENT

[character] STAN
[dialogue] What do you think she’s thinking?

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Maybe she expects her son to just 
[dialogue] walk out of the school with the 
[dialogue] rest of us.
[page_number] 10.

[character] RICHIE
[dialogue] Yeah right, Dorsey Coen’s been 
[dialogue] living in home ec these last three 
[dialogue] weeks.

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] They’re not going to find him.

[character] RICHIE
[dialogue] Sure they will. In a ditch. All 
[dialogue] decomposed, covered in worms and 
[dialogue] maggots and smelling like your 
[dialogue] Mom’s--

[action] Richie is CHECKED TO THE PAVEMENT by TRAVIS BOWERS (16), a 
[action] sadistic tower of muscle. His glasses fall off and a pile of 
[action] comics and video game magazines spill from his back pack.

[character] TRAVIS
[dialogue] Sorry chode. Didn’t see you.

[action] Travis kicks the glasses away. His thug sidekicks laugh: 
[action] SNATCH HUGGINS (15), PATRICK HOCKSTETTLER (17) and VICTOR 
[action] CRISS (15) -- one a lunkhead oaf, another a perpetually 
[action] giggly fire-starting sociopath, and the last one a scrap and 
[action] scab junk yard dog type. 

[action] Victor grabs Stan’s yarmulka and tosses it into the window of 
[action] a departing bus.

[character] VICTOR
[dialogue] Frisbee, fuck nut.

[action] Snatch burps in Eddie’s face. Eddie wilts under the smell. 
[action] Will scoops up the remains of Richie’s glasses, smashed by 
[action] the bus.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] You suck, Bowers!

[action] Travis and his goons turn. They glare at Will menacingly.

[character] TRAVIS
[dialogue] You say something?

[action] Everyone around them stops and watches, waiting for Will to 
[action] respond. Eddie gives Will a look to shut it. He does.

[character] TRAVIS (CONT’D)
[dialogue] Thought so. You got a free ride 
[dialogue] this year because of your little 
[dialogue] brother. But ride’s over, 
[dialogue] Denbrough. 
[page_number] 11.

[] (MORE)

[character] TRAVIS (CONT’D) (CONT'D)
[dialogue] This summer’s gonna be a hurt 
[dialogue] train, for you and your faggot 
[dialogue] friends. 
[dialogue] (to Snatch and Victor)
[dialogue] Let’s roll. We got a lard ass to 
[dialogue] find.

[scene_heading] EXT. EMERGENCY EXIT - DERRY HIGH SCHOOL - DAY

[action] A door opens by the dumpsters and out peaks BEN HANSCOM (13), 
[action] the “lard ass” in question, making sure the coast is clear. 
[action] He wears an oversized sweatshirt and a pair of girly high-
[action] waisted blue jeans.

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] Soooo, you gonna let me go by or is 
[dialogue] there a secret password or 
[dialogue] something?

[action] He turns to see Beverly.

[character] BEN
[dialogue] Sorry.

[action] He steps aside. Beverly tip-toes past, lighting a cigarette 
[action] like a pro.

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] “Sorry”’s not much of a password. 
[dialogue] Now “kittyhawk,” that’s a good one.

[character] BEN
[dialogue] Ballyhoo?

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] Riproar.

[character] BEN
[dialogue] Barnburner.

[action] Beverly smiles and offers Ben a smoke. He refuses. She shrugs 
[action] and blows a smoke ring in his face. 

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] If you’re worried about Bowers and 
[dialogue] his merry band of a-holes hassling 
[dialogue] you, don’t. I saw them all leave 
[dialogue] out front.

[character] BEN
[dialogue] Thanks.
[page_number] 12.

[character] TRAVIS (CONT’D)

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] You’re the new kid right? I’m--

[character] BEN
[parenthetical] (blurting)
[dialogue] Beverly Marsh.

[action] A little too quick. His ears turn red with embarrassment.

[character] BEN (CONT’D)
[dialogue] I just know ‘cuz we were in social 
[dialogue] studies.  Together, in the same 
[dialogue] class.  You know, like the 
[dialogue] constitution test, and stuff. 
[dialogue] (realizing he’s vomiting 
[dialogue] nonsense)
[dialogue] I’m Ben.

[action] Bev smiles, knowingly. It’s clear he likes her. Ben notices a 
[action] FAINT YELLOW BRUISE on her forearm. Bev notices him noticing.

[character] BEVERLY
[parenthetical] (off Ben’s look)
[dialogue] I slipped in the shower. What’s 
[dialogue] your excuse?

[character] BEN
[dialogue] I fell on three boxes of donuts.

[action] Beverly laughs.

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] Well, see ya around Ben from sosh 
[dialogue] class. “Get laid in the shade.”

[character] BEN
[dialogue] Uh, you too, Beverly. “Stay cool.”

[action] Ben watches her go, totally smitten.

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] K.I.T.

[scene_heading] INT. WILL DENBROUGH’S HOUSE - DAY

[action] Dust collects on the piano in the living room.  A stale quiet 
[action] suffocates the house.  The only sound the ticking of a clock 
[action] and the distant yelling of playing children.
[page_number] 13.

[scene_heading] INT. WILL DENBROUGH’S HOUSE - WILL’S ROOM - SAME

[action] Will Denbrough laces his shoes.  As he leaves his room, he 
[action] notices the door to George’s room is open, his FATHER, ZACH 
[action] DENBROUGH (30s), lost in thought notices him.  

[character] ZACH
[parenthetical] (without emphasis)
[dialogue] Hey Champ.  Going out to play?

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Yeah, Dad.

[action] Will looks like he wants to say more, but doesn’t have the 
[action] words to articulate it.  

[scene_heading] INT. WILL DENBROUGH’S HOUSE, LIVING ROOM - DAY

[action] Jumping down the stairs and rounding the corner, he sees his 
[action] catatonic Mother, silhouetted by the midday light, sitting in 
[action] the darkness, staring blankly at the piano.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Hi, Ma.

[action] Growing accustomed to his parent’s lack of communication, he 
[action] sifts through a PILE OF UNOPENED MAIL on the dining table and 
[action] finds his report card.  Opens it.  All A’s.

[character] WILL (CONT’D)
[dialogue] I got my report card.

[action] He holds the paper limply in his hands.  She doesn’t 
[action] acknowledge him and instead gets up and slides the doors shut 
[action] to the piano room.

[scene_heading] INT. EDDIE KASPBRAK’S HOUSE - DAY

[action] Eddie, Richie, Will, and Stan scramble in the front door like 
[action] feral animals. An enormous 300-pound woman sits in the living 
[action] room doing a puzzle on her own stomach. SONIA KASPBRAK (40s).

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] Hi Mom.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Mrs. Kaspbrak.

[character] RICHIE
[dialogue] Ma’am.
[page_number] 14.

[scene_heading] INT. EDDIE’S KITCHEN - DAY

[action] They raid the kitchen, stuffing Capri Suns, Ding Dongs, 
[action] whatever snacks they can find into their backpacks. Mrs. 
[action] Kaspbrak calls from the room.

[character] MRS. KASPBRAK
[dialogue] Eddie-bear, where are you boys off 
[dialogue] to?

[character] RICHIE
[dialogue] Eddie-bear?

[action] Will elbows him.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Just my backyard, Mrs. K. We got a 
[dialogue] new, uh, badminton set.

[character] MRS. KASPBRAK
[dialogue] Okay.  Just don’t go rolling around 
[dialogue] too much on that grass, sweetie.  
[dialogue] Especially if it’s just been cut.  
[dialogue] You know how your allergies get.

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] Yes, ma.

[character] MRS. KASPBRAK
[dialogue] Isn’t he cute, boys? So  cute .

[action] She wiggles her fat toes at him. He’s mortified.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Cutest boy we know, Mrs. Kaspbrak.

[character] RICHIE
[dialogue] The Miss America of cute boys. 
[dialogue] Seriously.

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] Bye ma.

[action] Fast as they came the boys blow out the front door.

[scene_heading] EXT. STREET IN FRONT OF LIBRARY - DAY

[action] Will, Rich, and Eddie speed past the library.
[page_number] 15.

[scene_heading] INT. PUBLIC LIBRARY - DAY

[action] All green globes, curving iron staircases, and shadowy charm. 
[action] A librarian, MRS. STARRET (50s) places a stack of books in 
[action] front of Ben.

[character] MRS. STARRET
[dialogue] Isn’t it summer vacation?  I would 
[dialogue] think you’d be ready to take a 
[dialogue] break from the books.

[character] BEN

[scene_heading] INT. DERRY SYNOGOGUE, DOWNSTAIRS - MOMENTS LATER

[action] Stanley walks down the old, molding stone stairs to the

[action] spirals into the deeper foundations of the building.  

[action] He can’t find the bathroom, but he does see the Mikveh--a 
[action] cleansing room for the women of a synogogue during their 
[action] monthlies.  It drips water and is lit by a spare light bulb.

[scene_heading] INT. SYNOGOGUE, MIKVEH - SAME

[action] Stanley walks in, knowing no one is watching him and unzips 
[action] his pants to relieve himself in the pool of water.

[action] Suddenly, he notices something bubbling from below.  A NAKED 
[action] WOMAN, her pale white skin glowing from the light.

[action] He backs away and quickly zips up his pants.

[action] The woman rises to the surface, exhaling a long held breath

[action] her butt shredded and rotten.

[action] been reduced to bones and gristle.

[dialogue] me.

[action] ominous figure is just ducking out of sight.

[page_number] 28.

[centered] AN EXPLOSION TOOK THE LIVES 
[centered] OF 102 SOULS (88 CHILDREN) 
[centered] EASTER SUNDAY, 1906.

[action] still leaned against it. It seems a million miles off.

[action] handhold and sways over its cavernous depth.

[action] again.

[action] another fire ball.  For a second we see the faces of DEAD 
[action] CHILDREN amongst the monoliths.  

[action] Hockstettler isn’t sure if he’s seen right. He walks in, the 
[action] hair rising on the back of his neck.

[action] Hockstettler carefully maneuvers his way down into the hold.  
[action] His feet, blindly tapping for purchase.

[character] HOCKSTETTLER
[dialogue] I ain’t afraid.  Any you ghosts 
[dialogue] down here can’t hurt me.

[action] As if it were a mantra, to make himself feel less scared.

[character] HOCKSTETTLER (CONT’D)
[dialogue] I seen ghosts.  I did them in their 
[dialogue] butt holes.  

[action] And out of the darkness...

[character] PENNYWISE
[dialogue] Did they float?

[action] He freezes.  Did he hear that or was that in his head?

[character] HOCKSTETTLER
[dialogue] I, I-  Who’s there?

[action] Hockstettler lights a ball of hair spray, it illuminates the 
[action] walls near him, but nothing far away.

[character] HOCKSTETTLER (CONT’D)
[dialogue] There’s no one down here.  There’s 
[dialogue] no one that can hurt me.

[action] He moves down deeper.  Lights the hair spray again, and for 
[action] second, we think we glimpsed, deeper in the shadows, 
[action] Pennywise.

[character] PENNWISE
[parenthetical] (whispering)
[dialogue] Patrick... Answer me.
[page_number] 31.

[action] Now sure he’s heard something, Patrick stops.  His breathing 
[action] starts to rapidly increase.  He wants to back up, but he’s 
[action] frozen.

[action] He lifts up his hair spray can, finger shaking on the plastic 
[action] nozzle, he lifts the lighter.  CLINK.  The flint lights up 
[action] the small flame.  Sputtering in the flowing air in the 
[action] cellar.  His breathing is arrhythmic, compressed.

[character] PENNYWISE
[dialogue] Because down here, we all float.

[action] He sprays.  The flame spits out and out of the darkness, 
[action] PENNYWISE the clown leaps onto Hockstettler, tumbling him 
[action] into the cellar hold, he drops the lighter and hairspray, 
[action] which rolls down to the feet of a child.  A DEAD CHILD.

[scene_heading] EXT. KEENE’S PHARMACY - DAY

[action] The pack of boys, Will, Richie, Ben, and Eddie come biking 
[action] down Center Street and swing into RICHARD’S ALLEY. They jump 
[action] off, bikes clattering to the pavement.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Okay Richie, you and...

[character] BEN
[dialogue] Ben.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Ben stay here. We’ll get some 
[dialogue] bandages and stuff.

[action] Will and Eddie race in, leaving Ben and Richie in the alley 
[action] which is emblazoned with a COLORFUL MURAL about the FBI’s 
[action] ambush of the infamous Bradley Gang, a celebrated slice of 
[action] Derry town history.

[character] BEN
[dialogue] Thanks for helping me.

[character] RICHIE
[dialogue] You’re not the only one on Bower’s 
[dialogue] shitlist.

[action] Ben nods, grateful for the company. He notices in the mural, 
[action] in one of the windows, a CLOWN watches the ambush with glee.

[scene_heading] INT. KEENE’S PHARMACY - DAY

[action] Eddie snatches cotton balls and antiseptic and bandages off 
[action] the shelf like an expert.  He checks the prices.
[page_number] 32.

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] Woah, that’s a lot of money. Will?

[action] Will pulls out one crumbled dollar.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] All I got.  You got an account here 
[dialogue] don’t you?

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] You crazy? My mom finds out I 
[dialogue] bought this stuff for myself I’ll 
[dialogue] have to spend the whole weekend in 
[dialogue] the emergency room getting x-rayed.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Well we need to do something. That 
[dialogue] kid out there looks like someone 
[dialogue] killed him.

[action] They glance at the mirror where MR. KEENE (70s) the grumpy, 
[action] eagle-eyed pharmacist watches them like a hawk.

[character] BEVERLY (O.S.)
[dialogue] You consider a diversion?

[action] They all turn to Beverly, who stands there sly and smiling.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Huh?

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] Your friend there. He has that 
[dialogue] thingy he always carries around in 
[dialogue] his cute little fannypack right?

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] My EpiPen Jr.?

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] Yeah. For when he spazzes out.

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] I don’t spaz. Those are allergic 
[dialogue] reactions. Life threatening 
[dialogue] allergic reactions. 

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] Whatever. The point is he has a 
[dialogue] track record right? Of spazzing 
[dialogue] out.

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] I don’t--
[page_number] 33.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] Eds, shut up. She’s on to 
[dialogue] something.

[scene_heading] INT. SAME - MOMENTS LATER - DAY

[action] Mr. Keene fills prescriptions. Eddie flops out of the aisle

[character] BEVERLY

[dialogue] that granola.  Mr. Keene!

[character] MR. KEENE

[character] WILL
[dialogue] I’m a pacifist.

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] My mom doesn’t like me handling 
[dialogue] needles.

[action] Annoyed, Keene comes out from behind the counter.  He kneels 
[action] next to Eddie and takes the pen, plunging it into his thigh.  
[action] Will screens Beverly as she SHOPLIFTS THE SUPPLIES and SLIPS 
[action] OUT THE STORE. Eddie opens his eyes, breathing normal.

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] Thanks, Mr. Keene.  That one was a 
[dialogue] real shit kicker.

[scene_heading] EXT. KEENE’S PHARMACY - DAY

[action] Will and Eddie race out laughing.  Bev is waiting on the curb 
[action] to meet them.  She flips Will the stolen goods.

[character] BEVERLY
[dialogue] Not bad for a bunch of amateurs.

[action] She flashes a pack of STOLEN CIGARETTES and struts off 
[action] towards her house.  THEY LOVE THIS GIRL.  Will closes Eddie’s 
[action] mouth and they hustle round the corner into RICHARD’S ALLEY.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] We got it!
[page_number] 34.

[action] He flips Ben his medicine. Stan runs up from behind, still 
[action] looking blanched from his synogogue experience.

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] Shit Stan, what happened to you?  
[dialogue] You look like you’ve seen a ghost.

[character] STAN
[dialogue] Nothing.

[action] He sees Ben there, looking like a train wreck.

[scene_heading] INT. POLICE STATION - DAY

[action] ANGLE ON A CLOSE-UP of the “Coen shoe” placed on a desk in

[scene_heading] INT. POLICE STATION - MAIN BULL PEN - SAME

[action] Ben, all bandaged up, looks on with Eddie, Richie, and Stan

[character] BEN
[dialogue] Beverly was there?

[character] EDDIE
[dialogue] Yeah.

[character] BEN

[action] Stanley wants Ben to shut up, but Ben is beyond excited.

[dialogue] alive man.

[dialogue] the kid’s shoe. It’s riveting.

[dialogue] be late for curfew.

[action] The boys shove him playfully.  Ben shoves back. 

[character] BEN
[dialogue] It’s funny, ‘til the last week I 
[dialogue] didn’t think anyone knew my name.

[character] RICHIE
[dialogue] You’re pretty friggin’ hard to 
[dialogue] miss.

[dialogue] Dorsey might of, I dunno... got

[scene_heading] INT. WILL’S HOUSE - NIGHT

[action] Will lays out on the carpet in front of the TV, eating ice 
[action] cream from a carton. His parents sit on opposite ends of the 
[action] couch like bookends, father flips through a  Popular 
[action] Mechanics . His mom stares at the TV, her mind elsewhere. 
[action] There seems to be no connection between any of them.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] I found something today.

[action] No response. Finally his Mom stirs.

[character] SHARON
[dialogue] You say something honey?

[character] WILL
[dialogue] That missing Coen boy. I found a 
[dialogue] clue.
[page_number] 37.

[action] His parents flash looks.

[character] ZACH
[dialogue] Stay out of it, Will. Let the 
[dialogue] police handle these things.

[action] They go back to watching TV. Will tries for some sort of 
[action] connection.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] What about Arcadia?

[action] His dad stops mid flip, all the air going out of the room.

[character] WILL (CONT’D)
[dialogue] We haven’t talked about our park 
[dialogue] trip at the end of the summer. 
[dialogue] Usually by now we’re all looking at 
[dialogue] the brochure together. We have so 
[dialogue] much fun there every--

[action] Will’s dad throws the magazine down.

[character] ZACH
[dialogue] Enough.

[action] He storms out to his workshop. Sharon is too upset to look 
[action] her son in the eye.

[character] WILL
[dialogue] What did I say?
